items.title: GST Cancellation SCN for NIL Returns: Is Such Notice Legally Valid?
items.summary: Can GST registration be cancelled merely because a taxpayer filed NIL returns for four consecutive tax periods? Examine the legality of GST cancellation show cause notices based solely on NIL turnover, the requirements of Section 29 of the CGST Act, and whether such automated notices satisfy the principles of natural justice. The post GST Cancellation SCN for NIL Returns: Is Such Notice Legally Valid? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has undergone significant developments since its inception. One crucial aspect of GST is the registration and cancellation of registrations. Section 29 of the CGST Act deals with the cancellation of registration, which can be initiated by the taxpayer or the proper officer. The GST law also mandates that every registered person shall furnish returns in the prescribed manner, which includes filing NIL returns if there are no transactions during a tax period. However, a recent trend has been observed where GST registrations are being cancelled solely based on the filing of NIL returns for consecutive tax periods, raising questions about the legality and validity of such cancellation show cause notices (SCNs).

The CGST Act, under Section 29(2), specifies the circumstances under which a registration can be cancelled by the proper officer. These include cases where the registered person has contravened any provision of the Act or the rules made thereunder, or where the registration has been obtained by means of fraud, willful misstatement, or suppression of facts. Furthermore, Section 29(3) requires that before cancelling the registration, the proper officer shall issue a notice to the registered person requiring him to show cause as to why his registration should not be cancelled. This notice must be issued following the principles of natural justice, which include giving the taxpayer an opportunity to be heard.

The GST Council and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) have also issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions related to registration and cancellation. For instance, Circular No. 95/14/2019-GST dated March 28, 2019, clarifies the procedure for cancellation of registration. Understanding these statutory provisions and clarifications is essential to assess the validity of cancellation SCNs issued based solely on the filing of NIL returns.

The Development in Detail

A significant development in the GST regime is the issuance of automated cancellation SCNs to taxpayers who have filed NIL returns for four consecutive tax periods. This has raised concerns among taxpayers and practitioners about the legitimacy of such notices. The primary question is whether the mere filing of NIL returns can be a valid ground for initiating cancellation proceedings under Section 29 of the CGST Act. The rationale behind this move seems to be the assumption that if a taxpayer has not reported any transactions over a period, their registration may no longer be required or could be a case of non-compliance.

However, this approach overlooks the fact that there could be genuine reasons for a taxpayer to file NIL returns, such as a temporary cessation of business activities due to market conditions, restructuring, or simply because the taxpayer is not required to report any transactions during that period. The automated issuance of SCNs without considering these nuances may lead to unnecessary litigation and compliance burdens on taxpayers. It is crucial to examine whether such an approach aligns with the principles of natural justice and the specific requirements of Section 29 of the CGST Act.

The development also highlights the need for a more nuanced approach to handling NIL returns and registration cancellations. Instead of relying solely on automated systems, tax authorities could consider a more holistic review of the taxpayer's compliance history and current business status before initiating cancellation proceedings. This could involve a preliminary scrutiny to identify cases where cancellation might be genuinely warranted due to non-compliance or fraud, distinguishing them from cases where NIL returns are filed for legitimate reasons.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 29 of the CGST Act is pivotal in understanding the legal framework surrounding the cancellation of GST registrations. Specifically, Section 29(1) allows a registered person to apply for cancellation of registration in certain circumstances, while Section 29(2) empowers the proper officer to cancel the registration on his own motion under specified conditions. The key provision in the context of NIL returns is the requirement under Section 29(3) that no registration shall be cancelled without giving the person an opportunity of being heard.

The CBIC has also issued clarifications and guidelines on the procedure for cancellation of registration, emphasizing the importance of adhering to the principles of natural justice. For instance, any notice issued for cancellation must clearly state the grounds on which the cancellation is proposed, giving the taxpayer adequate time to respond and be heard. This underscores the need for a fair and transparent process in handling cancellation proceedings, including those initiated due to the filing of NIL returns.

Furthermore, the GST law and rules prescribe the manner in which returns are to be furnished, including NIL returns. The GST Council has also been proactive in addressing taxpayer concerns and clarifying procedural aspects through notifications and circulars. For example, notifications may specify the conditions under which a registered person is required to file returns, even if no tax is payable. Understanding these provisions is crucial for assessing the validity of cancellation SCNs based on NIL returns and for ensuring compliance with GST regulations.

Critical Analysis

The critical analysis of the situation reveals that while the intention behind the automated issuance of cancellation SCNs for NIL returns might be to streamline compliance and reduce non-active registrations, the approach may not be entirely aligned with the statutory framework and principles of natural justice. The GST Act and rules provide for specific grounds for cancellation, which must be based on a genuine reason and not merely on the absence of transactions reported in returns.

A closer examination of Section 29 of the CGST Act and relevant circulars and notifications suggests that cancellation proceedings should be initiated based on tangible evidence of non-compliance or fraud, rather than the mere filing of NIL returns. This requires a more detailed scrutiny of the taxpayer's situation, which automated systems may not adequately provide. Moreover, the principles of natural justice demand that taxpayers are given a fair opportunity to present their case before any adverse action is taken, which may not be fully satisfied by automated notices.

From a legal standpoint, the validity of such cancellation SCNs can be challenged on the grounds that they do not adhere to the requirements of Section 29(3) of the CGST Act, which mandates an opportunity of being heard. Furthermore, the approach may be seen as overly broad and not tailored to the specific circumstances of each taxpayer, potentially leading to unnecessary disputes and litigation. A more nuanced and taxpayer-centric approach, considering the genuine reasons for filing NIL returns, would be more appropriate and in line with the GST law's intent to facilitate ease of doing business.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and practitioners. It is essential for taxpayers who have received cancellation SCNs based on NIL returns to carefully review the notice and respond appropriately. Here are key action points for practitioners and taxpayers:

1. Review the Notice Carefully: Ensure that the notice specifies clear grounds for cancellation and provides an opportunity for the taxpayer to be heard. If the notice is based solely on NIL returns without considering the taxpayer's specific circumstances, it may be challenged.

2. Respond to the Notice: It is crucial to respond to the cancellation SCN within the specified timeframe, providing detailed explanations for the filing of NIL returns and any supporting documentation that may be relevant.

3. Seek Clarification: If the taxpayer is unclear about the grounds for cancellation or the procedure being followed, they should seek clarification from the tax authorities. This can help in understanding the basis of the notice and in preparing an appropriate response.

4. Maintain Detailed Records: Taxpayers should maintain detailed records of their business activities, including reasons for filing NIL returns. This documentation can be crucial in supporting their response to the cancellation SCN and in any subsequent litigation.

5. Consult with Tax Professionals: Given the legal complexities involved, it is advisable for taxpayers to consult with tax professionals who can provide guidance on responding to the notice and navigating the cancellation proceedings. Professionals can help in ensuring that the taxpayer's rights are protected and that they are in compliance with all GST regulations.

items.title: Salary Taxable Only on Accrual, Not Mere Form 16 or 26AS Entries: Bengaluru ITAT
items.summary: ITAT Bangalore held disputed salary is taxable only if it became due and remanded the matter to verify the employment terms and employer's claim. The post Salary Taxable Only on Accrual, Not Mere Form 16 or 26AS Entries: Bengaluru ITAT appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, governs the taxation of income in India, including salary income. Section 15 of the Act states that income from salary is taxable in the hands of the recipient. However, the Act also provides for the timing of recognition of such income, which is crucial in determining the taxable amount. Section 145A of the Income Tax Act deals with the method of accounting, which can be either mercantile or cash basis. The mercantile system of accounting recognizes income on an accrual basis, whereas the cash system recognizes income on a cash basis.

In the context of salary income, the Supreme Court has held in various judgments that salary is taxable on an accrual basis, meaning it is taxable when it becomes due, regardless of when it is actually paid. This principle is also reflected in Section 192 of the Act, which deals with the deduction of tax at source from salary. The section requires the employer to deduct tax at source when the salary is paid, but the tax liability arises when the salary becomes due.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and notifications to clarify the taxation of salary income. For instance, Circular No. 9/2017 dated March 23, 2017, clarifies the taxation of salary income in the case of employees who are posted abroad. The circular provides that salary income is taxable in India if it is earned for services rendered in India, regardless of where the employee is posted.

Facts of the Case

The present case before the Bengaluru ITAT involved a dispute over the taxation of salary income. The taxpayer had received a Form 16 and Form 26AS from the employer, which showed a certain amount of salary income. However, the taxpayer disputed the amount, claiming that the salary had not become due and was therefore not taxable. The Assessing Officer (AO) rejected the taxpayer's claim and held that the salary was taxable based on the Form 16 and Form 26AS entries.

The taxpayer appealed to the ITAT, which remanded the matter to the AO to verify the employment terms and the employer's claim. The ITAT held that the disputed salary was taxable only if it became due, and not merely based on the Form 16 or Form 26AS entries. This decision highlights the importance of verifying the facts and circumstances of each case, rather than relying solely on documentary evidence.

The facts of the case also highlight the complexities of salary taxation, particularly in cases where the employer and employee may have different interpretations of the employment terms. The ITAT's decision emphasizes the need for clarity and precision in determining the taxable amount, and the importance of considering the accrual basis of taxation.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the ITAT was whether the disputed salary was taxable based on the Form 16 and Form 26AS entries, or whether it was taxable only when it became due. The taxpayer argued that the salary had not become due and was therefore not taxable, while the AO contended that the salary was taxable based on the documentary evidence.

The ITAT had to consider the principles of accrual basis of taxation, as well as the provisions of the Income Tax Act, to determine the taxable amount. The tribunal also had to consider the facts and circumstances of the case, including the employment terms and the employer's claim, to arrive at a decision.

The issue before the ITAT is significant, as it highlights the complexities of salary taxation and the need for clarity and precision in determining the taxable amount. The decision of the ITAT has implications for taxpayers and employers, and emphasizes the importance of understanding the accrual basis of taxation and the provisions of the Income Tax Act.

Arguments & Contentions

The taxpayer argued that the salary had not become due and was therefore not taxable. The taxpayer contended that the Form 16 and Form 26AS entries were not conclusive evidence of the taxable amount, and that the AO should have considered the employment terms and the employer's claim before determining the taxable amount.

The AO, on the other hand, argued that the salary was taxable based on the documentary evidence, including the Form 16 and Form 26AS entries. The AO contended that the taxpayer had not provided any evidence to support the claim that the salary had not become due, and that the AO had correctly determined the taxable amount based on the available evidence.

The ITAT considered the arguments and contentions of both parties, and held that the disputed salary was taxable only if it became due. The tribunal emphasized the importance of verifying the facts and circumstances of each case, and the need for clarity and precision in determining the taxable amount.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT held that the disputed salary was taxable only if it became due, and not merely based on the Form 16 or Form 26AS entries. The tribunal remanded the matter to the AO to verify the employment terms and the employer's claim, and to determine the taxable amount based on the accrual basis of taxation.

The ITAT's ruling is significant, as it emphasizes the importance of understanding the accrual basis of taxation and the provisions of the Income Tax Act. The decision highlights the need for clarity and precision in determining the taxable amount, and the importance of considering the facts and circumstances of each case.

The tribunal's reasoning is based on the principles of accrual basis of taxation, as well as the provisions of the Income Tax Act. The ITAT considered the arguments and contentions of both parties, and applied the relevant legal principles to arrive at a decision. The decision is a significant contribution to the jurisprudence on salary taxation, and provides guidance to taxpayers and employers on the accrual basis of taxation.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The ITAT's decision has significant implications for practitioners, including the following:

1. **Verify employment terms**: Practitioners should verify the employment terms and the employer's claim to determine the taxable amount. This includes reviewing the employment contract, the terms of service, and any other relevant documentation.

2. **Understand accrual basis of taxation**: Practitioners should understand the accrual basis of taxation, including the principles of Section 145A of the Income Tax Act. This includes recognizing income on an accrual basis, rather than a cash basis.

3. **Consider Form 16 and Form 26AS entries**: Practitioners should consider the Form 16 and Form 26AS entries, but should not rely solely on these documents to determine the taxable amount. Instead, practitioners should verify the facts and circumstances of each case to determine the taxable amount.

4. **Maintain accurate documentation**: Practitioners should maintain accurate documentation, including employment contracts, terms of service, and other relevant documents. This documentation is critical in determining the taxable amount and in supporting the taxpayer's claim.

5. **Seek professional advice**: Practitioners should seek professional advice if they are unsure about the taxation of salary income or the accrual basis of taxation. This includes consulting with tax professionals, including chartered accountants and tax lawyers, to ensure compliance with the Income Tax Act and to minimize tax liabilities.

items.title: Search Assessments Restored Despite Non-Compliance, Subject to ₹50,000 Cost: Bengaluru ITAT
items.summary: ITAT Bangalore restored six Section 153A search assessment appeals to the AO, subject to ₹50,000 cost per appeal for repeated non-compliance. The post Search Assessments Restored Despite Non-Compliance, Subject to ₹50,000 Cost: Bengaluru ITAT appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the Income Tax Department to conduct searches and seizures under Section 132, which can lead to the issuance of a notice under Section 153A for the assessment of income escaping assessment. The assessment proceedings under Section 153A are governed by the provisions of Section 153A to Section 153G of the Income Tax Act. The CBDT has also issued various circulars and guidelines to ensure that the assessment proceedings are conducted in a fair and transparent manner. For instance, Circular No. 24/2015 dated July 31, 2015, provides guidance on the scope of assessment under Section 153A.

It is also relevant to note that the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of CIT vs. Kabul Chawla (2016) has held that the assessment under Section 153A can be made only in respect of the previous year(s) for which the search took place. The Court also emphasized the importance of following the due process of law and adhering to the principles of natural justice while conducting assessment proceedings. Furthermore, Section 254 of the Income Tax Act empowers the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) to pass orders as it thinks fit, which includes the power to restore an appeal to the file of the Assessing Officer (AO) with appropriate directions.

In the context of search assessments, the ITAT has consistently held that the AO must follow the procedure laid down under the Act and the rules made thereunder. The Tribunal has also emphasized the importance of adhering to the time limits prescribed under the Act, including the time limit for completing the assessment proceedings under Section 153B. Non-compliance with these time limits can result in the assessment being rendered invalid, as held by the Hon'ble Delhi High Court in the case of Gee Vee Enterprises vs. Addl. CIT (2016).

Facts of the Case

The present case involves six appeals filed by the assessee before the ITAT, Bangalore, against the orders of the AO passed under Section 153A of the Income Tax Act. The AO had initiated assessment proceedings under Section 153A in consequence of a search and seizure operation conducted under Section 132 of the Act. However, the AO failed to comply with the directions of the Tribunal on multiple occasions, leading to a delay in the completion of the assessment proceedings.

The assessee had challenged the orders of the AO before the ITAT, contending that the assessment proceedings were barred by limitation and that the AO had failed to follow the due process of law. The ITAT, while considering the appeals, observed that the AO had repeatedly failed to comply with the directions of the Tribunal, which had resulted in a delay in the disposal of the appeals. The Tribunal also noted that the AO had not provided any reasonable explanation for the delay.

The ITAT, after considering the facts and circumstances of the case, decided to restore the appeals to the file of the AO, subject to the payment of a cost of ₹50,000 per appeal. The Tribunal held that the restoration of the appeals was necessary to ensure that the assessment proceedings were conducted in a fair and transparent manner and that the assessee was given an opportunity to present its case before the AO.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The primary issue before the ITAT was whether the assessment proceedings under Section 153A could be restored to the file of the AO despite the non-compliance of the AO with the directions of the Tribunal. The ITAT also had to consider whether the AO's failure to follow the due process of law and the delay in completing the assessment proceedings had resulted in a denial of justice to the assessee.

The ITAT had to balance the need to ensure that the assessment proceedings were conducted in a fair and transparent manner with the need to prevent unnecessary delays and hardships to the assessee. The Tribunal also had to consider the provisions of Section 254 of the Income Tax Act, which empowers the ITAT to pass orders as it thinks fit, including the power to restore an appeal to the file of the AO.

The ITAT also had to consider the principles of natural justice and the need to adhere to the due process of law, as enunciated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in various decisions, including the case of CIT vs. Kabul Chawla (2016). The Tribunal had to ensure that the assessee was given a fair opportunity to present its case before the AO and that the assessment proceedings were conducted in a manner that was consistent with the principles of fairness and transparency.

Arguments & Contentions

The assessee had contended before the ITAT that the assessment proceedings under Section 153A were barred by limitation and that the AO had failed to follow the due process of law. The assessee had also argued that the AO's failure to comply with the directions of the Tribunal had resulted in a delay in the completion of the assessment proceedings, which had caused undue hardship to the assessee.

The AO, on the other hand, had contended that the assessment proceedings under Section 153A were valid and that the delay in completing the assessment proceedings was due to circumstances beyond the control of the AO. The AO had also argued that the assessee had not been denied an opportunity to present its case before the AO and that the assessment proceedings had been conducted in a fair and transparent manner.

The ITAT, after considering the arguments and contentions of both parties, held that the assessment proceedings under Section 153A could be restored to the file of the AO, subject to the payment of a cost of ₹50,000 per appeal. The Tribunal also directed the AO to complete the assessment proceedings within a specified time limit and to provide the assessee with an opportunity to present its case before the AO.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT, in its ruling, held that the assessment proceedings under Section 153A could be restored to the file of the AO, despite the non-compliance of the AO with the directions of the Tribunal. The Tribunal observed that the AO's failure to comply with the directions of the Tribunal had resulted in a delay in the completion of the assessment proceedings, which had caused undue hardship to the assessee.

The ITAT also held that the restoration of the appeals to the file of the AO was necessary to ensure that the assessment proceedings were conducted in a fair and transparent manner. The Tribunal directed the AO to complete the assessment proceedings within a specified time limit and to provide the assessee with an opportunity to present its case before the AO.

The ITAT also imposed a cost of ₹50,000 per appeal on the AO, which was to be paid to the assessee. The Tribunal held that the imposition of cost was necessary to ensure that the AO complied with the directions of the Tribunal and to prevent unnecessary delays and hardships to the assessee. The Tribunal's ruling is consistent with the principles of natural justice and the need to adhere to the due process of law, as enunciated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in various decisions.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

Based on the ITAT's ruling, the following are the key takeaways for practitioners: 1. The AO must comply with the directions of the Tribunal and complete the assessment proceedings within the specified time limit to avoid delays and hardships to the assessee. 2. The assessee must ensure that it presents its case before the AO and avails of all opportunities to present its contentions and arguments. 3. The ITAT has the power to restore an appeal to the file of the AO, subject to the payment of a cost, to ensure that the assessment proceedings are conducted in a fair and transparent manner. 4. The imposition of cost by the ITAT is a deterrent to the AO to ensure compliance with the directions of the Tribunal and to prevent unnecessary delays and hardships to the assessee. 5. Practitioners must carefully review the assessment proceedings and ensure that the AO has followed the due process of law and the principles of natural justice, as enunciated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in various decisions.

items.title: Safari Retreats: Why SC ITC Relief on Malls, Hotels & Warehouses Short-Lived
items.summary: Article examines the Safari Retreats judgment, retrospective amendment to Section 17(5)(d), dismissal of the review petition, and construction-related ITC issues. The post Safari Retreats: Why SC ITC Relief on Malls, Hotels & Warehouses Short-Lived appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The GST regime, introduced by the CGST Act 2017, brought about a paradigm shift in the indirect tax landscape of India. One of the critical aspects of this regime is the availability of Input Tax Credit (ITC) to taxpayers. Section 16 of the CGST Act allows a registered person to claim ITC on the tax charged on supply of goods or services used in the course or furtherance of business. However, Section 17(5) of the CGST Act lists out certain supplies that are not eligible for ITC. Specifically, Section 17(5)(d) denies ITC on goods or services used for construction of immovable property, other than plant and machinery.

The Safari Retreats judgment, which is the focal point of this editorial, delves into the nuances of ITC eligibility on construction-related services. The Supreme Court's decision in this case has significant implications for taxpayers who have claimed ITC on such services. To understand the context, it is essential to refer to the CGST Act and the IGST Act, which collectively form the framework for GST in India. The Safari Retreats case has been a subject of interest among tax practitioners, given its potential impact on the construction and real estate sectors.

A thorough analysis of the GST legislation, including the CGST Act and the IGST Act, reveals that the lawmakers intended to restrict ITC on certain supplies to prevent misuse and ensure that the benefit of ITC is limited to genuine business expenses. The provisions of Section 17(5) are critical in this regard, as they explicitly deny ITC on specific supplies, including construction of immovable property. The Safari Retreats judgment must be seen in the light of these statutory provisions and the underlying legislative intent.

The Development in Detail

The Safari Retreats case involved a dispute regarding the eligibility of ITC on construction-related services. The taxpayer had claimed ITC on such services, which was disputed by the revenue authorities. The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the taxpayer, allowing the claim of ITC on construction-related services. However, this relief was short-lived, as the government subsequently introduced a retrospective amendment to Section 17(5)(d) of the CGST Act, effectively overturning the Supreme Court's decision.

The retrospective amendment has significant implications for taxpayers who had claimed ITC on construction-related services in reliance on the Safari Retreats judgment. The amendment denies ITC on such services, effectively rendering the Supreme Court's decision moot. The dismissal of the review petition filed against the Safari Retreats judgment has further cemented the position that the retrospective amendment would apply, denying ITC on construction-related services.

The development in the Safari Retreats case highlights the complexities and challenges of the GST regime. The introduction of retrospective amendments can have far-reaching consequences for taxpayers, who may have relied on judicial decisions or administrative guidance in claiming ITC. The Safari Retreats case serves as a reminder that the GST legislation is still evolving, and taxpayers must remain vigilant and adapt to changes in the law and its interpretation.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 17(5)(d) of the CGST Act is a critical provision that denies ITC on goods or services used for construction of immovable property, other than plant and machinery. The retrospective amendment to this provision has clarified that ITC would not be available on construction-related services. This clarification has significant implications for taxpayers who had claimed ITC on such services, as they may now be required to reverse the ITC claimed.

The CBIC has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions of the CGST Act, including those related to ITC. For instance, Circular No. 105/24/2019-GST dated 28.06.2019 provides guidance on the eligibility of ITC on construction-related services. However, the retrospective amendment to Section 17(5)(d) has overridden these clarifications, denying ITC on construction-related services.

The Safari Retreats judgment and the subsequent retrospective amendment have highlighted the need for taxpayers to carefully evaluate their ITC claims. The provisions of Section 17(5) must be carefully considered, and taxpayers must ensure that they are not claiming ITC on ineligible supplies. The CBIC's clarifications and guidance on ITC eligibility must also be taken into account, although the retrospective amendment has limited the scope of these clarifications.

Critical Analysis

The Safari Retreats judgment and the subsequent retrospective amendment have significant implications for taxpayers and the GST regime as a whole. The denial of ITC on construction-related services may lead to increased costs for taxpayers, particularly in the construction and real estate sectors. The retrospective amendment has also raised concerns about the stability and predictability of the GST regime, as taxpayers may be required to reverse ITC claims made in good faith.

The Supreme Court's decision in the Safari Retreats case was based on a nuanced interpretation of the CGST Act and the IGST Act. However, the retrospective amendment has effectively overturned this decision, highlighting the challenges of interpreting the GST legislation. The amendment has also underscored the need for taxpayers to remain vigilant and adapt to changes in the law and its interpretation.

The Safari Retreats case has significant implications for the construction and real estate sectors, which are critical components of the Indian economy. The denial of ITC on construction-related services may lead to increased costs and reduced competitiveness for businesses in these sectors. The government must carefully consider the impact of the retrospective amendment and provide guidance and support to taxpayers who may be affected by this change.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Safari Retreats judgment and the subsequent retrospective amendment have significant compliance implications for taxpayers. Taxpayers who had claimed ITC on construction-related services must carefully evaluate their ITC claims and reverse any ineligible claims. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Review ITC claims: Taxpayers must review their ITC claims to ensure that they are not claiming ITC on ineligible supplies, including construction-related services.

2. Reverse ineligible claims: Taxpayers who had claimed ITC on construction-related services must reverse these claims to avoid any potential penalties or interest.

3. Evaluate the impact of the retrospective amendment: Taxpayers must carefully evaluate the impact of the retrospective amendment on their business and take necessary steps to mitigate any adverse effects.

4. Seek professional advice: Taxpayers must seek professional advice to ensure that they are complying with the provisions of the CGST Act and the IGST Act, including the retrospective amendment to Section 17(5)(d).

5. Monitor developments: Taxpayers must monitor developments in the GST regime, including any further amendments or clarifications, to ensure that they are up-to-date with the latest developments and can adapt to any changes in the law or its interpretation.

items.title: Parallel Investigations Under Section 212: Can CBI Probe Alongside SFIO?
items.summary: Article discusses the pending Supreme Court challenge on SFIO and CBI jurisdiction under Section 212 of the Companies Act and related PMLA issues. The post Parallel Investigations Under Section 212: Can CBI Probe Alongside SFIO? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The jurisdictional debate between the Serious Fraud Investigation Office (SFIO) and the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) under Section 212 of the Companies Act, 2013, has sparked intense discussion among legal practitioners and corporate entities. Section 212 empowers the SFIO to investigate into the affairs of a company, while the CBI is entrusted with the responsibility of investigating into offences under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 (PMLA). The interplay between these two investigative agencies raises questions about the scope of their respective jurisdictions and the potential for parallel investigations. The Companies Act, 2013, and the PMLA, 2002, are the primary statutes governing the investigative powers of the SFIO and the CBI, respectively.

Section 212 of the Companies Act, 2013, provides that the SFIO shall investigate into the affairs of a company on a reference made by the Central Government. The SFIO is empowered to investigate into any matter relating to the company, including its formation, business, and financial transactions. On the other hand, the PMLA, 2002, empowers the CBI to investigate into offences of money laundering, which includes the acquisition, possession, or use of proceeds of crime. The CBI's investigative powers under the PMLA are governed by Section 44 of the Act, which provides that the CBI shall investigate into any offence under the PMLA.

The intersection of these two statutes raises questions about the jurisdictional boundaries between the SFIO and the CBI. The Supreme Court's decision in the case of Director, SFIO vs. Narayan Rao (2020) has provided some clarity on this issue. In this case, the Supreme Court held that the SFIO has the primary jurisdiction to investigate into the affairs of a company, while the CBI's jurisdiction is limited to investigating into offences under the PMLA. However, the Court also noted that there may be instances where the SFIO and the CBI may need to conduct parallel investigations, particularly in cases where there are allegations of money laundering.

The Development in Detail

The recent development in this area is the pending Supreme Court challenge to the jurisdiction of the SFIO and the CBI under Section 212 of the Companies Act. The challenge is based on the argument that the SFIO and the CBI are empowered to conduct parallel investigations, and that the SFIO's investigative powers are not limited to investigating into the affairs of a company. The petitioners have argued that the SFIO's powers under Section 212 are broader than those of the CBI under the PMLA, and that the SFIO should be allowed to investigate into offences under the PMLA as well.

The respondent, the Central Government, has argued that the SFIO's jurisdiction is limited to investigating into the affairs of a company, and that the CBI has the primary jurisdiction to investigate into offences under the PMLA. The Government has also argued that allowing the SFIO to investigate into offences under the PMLA would create confusion and overlap between the two investigative agencies. The Supreme Court's decision in this case is likely to have significant implications for the jurisdictional boundaries between the SFIO and the CBI.

The implications of the Supreme Court's decision will also extend to the application of Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, 2017, which provides that the jurisdiction of the tax authorities shall not be affected by any investigation or proceeding under any other law. This provision is relevant in cases where the SFIO or the CBI is investigating into the affairs of a company, and the tax authorities are also seeking to investigate into the company's tax affairs. The Supreme Court's decision will provide clarity on the interplay between these different investigative agencies and the tax authorities.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 212 of the Companies Act, 2013, is the key provision governing the SFIO's investigative powers. This section provides that the SFIO shall investigate into the affairs of a company on a reference made by the Central Government. The section also empowers the SFIO to investigate into any matter relating to the company, including its formation, business, and financial transactions. The PMLA, 2002, is the primary statute governing the CBI's investigative powers, and Section 44 of the Act provides that the CBI shall investigate into any offence under the PMLA.

The impact of the Supreme Court's decision will be significant, as it will provide clarity on the jurisdictional boundaries between the SFIO and the CBI. If the Court holds that the SFIO and the CBI can conduct parallel investigations, it will create a new paradigm for investigative agencies in India. On the other hand, if the Court holds that the SFIO's jurisdiction is limited to investigating into the affairs of a company, it will create a clear distinction between the investigative powers of the SFIO and the CBI.

The decision will also have implications for the application of Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides that the income-tax authorities shall not be bound by any finding of fact or law made by any other authority. This provision is relevant in cases where the SFIO or the CBI is investigating into the affairs of a company, and the income-tax authorities are also seeking to investigate into the company's tax affairs. The Supreme Court's decision will provide clarity on the interplay between these different investigative agencies and the income-tax authorities.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the issue reveals that the jurisdictional debate between the SFIO and the CBI is complex and multifaceted. On one hand, the SFIO has the primary jurisdiction to investigate into the affairs of a company, while the CBI has the primary jurisdiction to investigate into offences under the PMLA. On the other hand, there may be instances where the SFIO and the CBI need to conduct parallel investigations, particularly in cases where there are allegations of money laundering.

The Supreme Court's decision in the case of Director, SFIO vs. Narayan Rao (2020) provides some clarity on this issue, but the pending challenge to the jurisdiction of the SFIO and the CBI under Section 212 of the Companies Act raises new questions. items.title: GST 2.0 & IMS: How to Claim ITC Without Getting Blocked

items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced in 2017, has undergone significant transformations to streamline its operations and plug loopholes. One of the critical components of GST is the Input Tax Credit (ITC) mechanism, which allows businesses to claim credits for taxes paid on inputs. Section 16 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), outlines the eligibility and conditions for claiming ITC. However, to ensure the authenticity of ITC claims and prevent fraud, the GST Council has introduced various measures, including the Invoice Matching System (IMS) and GST 2.0, which aims to make the GST system more robust and transparent.

Section 41 of the CGST Act provides for the matching of input tax credit claims with the corresponding output tax liability of the supplier. This provision is crucial in preventing fake invoicing and ensuring that only genuine ITC claims are allowed. The concept of deemed acceptance, as outlined in Section 36A of the CGST Act, also plays a significant role in this context, as it provides that if a recipient does not communicate any discrepancy in the details of the invoice or debit note to the supplier within a specified timeframe, such details shall be deemed to be accepted by the recipient.

The GST Council's efforts to strengthen the ITC mechanism are also reflected in the various circulars and notifications issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC). For instance, Circular No. 123/42/2019-GST, dated 11th November 2019, clarifies the procedure for claiming ITC in cases where the supplier has not uploaded the invoice details. These developments underscore the importance of understanding the statutory framework and the nuances of ITC claims under GST.

The Development in Detail

The introduction of GST 2.0 and IMS marks a significant shift in the GST landscape, particularly with regard to ITC management. The IMS, on the other hand, is designed to facilitate the matching of invoices and ensure that only genuine ITC claims are allowed. To achieve this, taxpayers are required to undertake certain mandatory actions, including reconciling their GSTR-2B statements with their financial records and ensuring that all invoices are accurately uploaded and matched.

Failure to do so may result in the recipient being deemed to have accepted the invoice details, which could have significant implications for ITC claims. From a policy perspective, the introduction of GST 2.0 and IMS is a significant step towards enhancing the efficiency and transparency of the GST system. items.title: Gameskraft Judgment Decoded: GST on Online Gaming
items.summary: Article summarises the Supreme Court's Gameskraft ruling on GST, actionable claims, valuation, supplier, taxable event and retrospective 2023 amendments. The post Gameskraft Judgment Decoded: GST on Online Gaming appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), has been a subject of continuous evolution, with the government and judiciary working in tandem to clarify and refine its provisions. One such area of clarification is the taxation of online gaming, which has gained significant attention in recent times. The CGST Act, specifically Section 9(1), imposes a tax on all intra-state supplies of goods or services, while the IGST Act, under Section 5, imposes a tax on all inter-state supplies of goods or services. The valuation of supplies, as per Section 15 of the CGST Act, is another critical aspect that determines the tax liability.

The online gaming industry, characterized by its intangible nature and cross-border transactions, poses unique challenges for tax authorities. The concept of "supply" under GST, as defined in Schedule II of the CGST Act, includes various activities such as the provision of services. However, the specific inclusion of online gaming within the ambit of taxable supplies has been a subject of debate. The GST Council, in its efforts to provide clarity, has issued notifications and circulars to guide the industry. For instance, the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) has issued circulars to clarify the taxability of certain services, including online gaming.

Furthermore, the Income Tax Act, 1961, particularly Section 145A, which deals with the method of accounting, also has a bearing on the GST implications for online gaming. The interplay between direct tax laws and indirect tax laws is crucial, as it affects the overall tax liability of online gaming operators. The Supreme Court, in its landmark judgments, has also provided guidance on the interpretation of tax laws, including the GST provisions. The recent judgment in the Gameskraft case is a significant development in this regard, as it sheds light on the tax implications of online gaming.

The Development in Detail

The Gameskraft judgment, a recent pronouncement by the Supreme Court, has far-reaching implications for the online gaming industry. The Court's decision revolves around the taxability of online gaming, specifically the classification of actionable claims, valuation, and the identification of the supplier. The judgment is based on the Court's interpretation of the CGST Act and the IGST Act, as well as relevant notifications and circulars issued by the CBIC. The Court's reasoning is rooted in the principles of tax law, including the concept of "supply" and the valuation provisions under GST.

The judgment delves into the nuances of online gaming, including the distinction between games of skill and games of chance. The Court's analysis is also informed by the retrospective amendments introduced in 2023, which have a significant impact on the tax liability of online gaming operators. The amendments, aimed at clarifying the taxability of online gaming, have introduced new provisions and modified existing ones, necessitating a re-evaluation of the industry's tax obligations.

The Gameskraft judgment is a culmination of the ongoing efforts to clarify the tax implications of online gaming. The Court's decision is expected to provide much-needed clarity and certainty to the industry, enabling operators to navigate the complex tax landscape with greater confidence. The judgment's implications extend beyond the online gaming industry, as it sets a precedent for the taxation of other intangible services and digital transactions.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The Gameskraft judgment provides clarity on several critical aspects of GST on online gaming, including the classification of actionable claims, valuation, and the identification of the supplier. The Court's interpretation of the CGST Act and the IGST Act has significant implications for the industry, as it affects the tax liability of online gaming operators. The judgment also sheds light on the retrospective amendments introduced in 2023, providing guidance on their applicability and impact on the industry.

The Court's analysis of the valuation provisions under GST is particularly noteworthy, as it highlights the complexities of determining the tax base for online gaming services. The judgment's emphasis on the distinction between games of skill and games of chance is also significant, as it affects the tax classification and liability of online gaming operators. The CBIC's circulars and notifications, which provide guidance on the taxability of online gaming, have also been considered by the Court in its judgment.

The Gameskraft judgment is a testament to the evolving nature of tax laws, particularly in the context of digital transactions and intangible services. The Court's decision demonstrates the importance of judicial interpretation in shaping the tax landscape, providing clarity and certainty to taxpayers and the industry at large. The judgment's implications will be closely watched by the online gaming industry, as well as other stakeholders, including tax authorities and policymakers.

Critical Analysis

The Gameskraft judgment is a landmark decision that has significant implications for the online gaming industry. The Court's interpretation of the CGST Act and the IGST Act provides much-needed clarity on the taxability of online gaming, including the classification of actionable claims, valuation, and the identification of the supplier. The judgment's emphasis on the distinction between games of skill and games of chance is also noteworthy, as it affects the tax classification and liability of online gaming operators.

However, the judgment also raises several questions and concerns, particularly with regard to the retrospective amendments introduced in 2023. The amendments, aimed at clarifying the taxability of online gaming, have introduced new provisions and modified existing ones, necessitating a re-evaluation of the industry's tax obligations. The Court's decision on the applicability and impact of these amendments will have significant implications for the industry, as it affects the tax liability of online gaming operators.

The Gameskraft judgment is also significant from a policy perspective, as it highlights the need for clarity and certainty in tax laws. The judgment demonstrates the importance of judicial interpretation in shaping the tax landscape, providing guidance to taxpayers and the industry at large. The Court's decision will inform future policy decisions, particularly with regard to the taxation of digital transactions and intangible services.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Gameskraft judgment has significant compliance implications for the online gaming industry. Online gaming operators must re-evaluate their tax obligations in light of the Court's decision, particularly with regard to the classification of actionable claims, valuation, and the identification of the supplier. The industry must also consider the retrospective amendments introduced in 2023, which affect the tax liability of online gaming operators.

To ensure compliance with the GST provisions, online gaming operators must take the following action points:

1. Re-evaluate their tax obligations in light of the Gameskraft judgment, particularly with regard to the classification of actionable claims, valuation, and the identification of the supplier.

2. Consider the retrospective amendments introduced in 2023 and their impact on the tax liability of online gaming operators.

3. Review their accounting and invoicing practices to ensure compliance with the GST provisions, including the valuation provisions under Section 15 of the CGST Act.

4. Seek professional advice from tax experts and consultants to ensure compliance with the GST provisions and to mitigate potential tax risks.

5. Monitor future developments and updates on the taxation of online gaming, including any changes to the GST provisions or notifications and circulars issued by the CBIC.

items.title: DGFT Syncs ITC (HS) Import Policy with Finance Act, 2026
items.summary: DGFT Notification No. 24/2026-27 amends ITC (HS) 2022 Schedule-I to align with the Finance Act, 2026, with immediate effect. The post DGFT Syncs ITC (HS) Import Policy with Finance Act, 2026 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian import policy is governed by the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), which operates under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry. The Importer-Exporter Code (IEC) is a key component of this framework, and importers must adhere to the provisions outlined in the ITC (HS) Schedule. The Finance Act, 2026, has introduced significant changes to the customs and taxation landscape, necessitating an alignment of the ITC (HS) import policy with these amendments. Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, 2017, and Section 5 of the IGST Act, 2017, provide the underlying framework for the taxation of imported goods.

The integration of the ITC (HS) import policy with the Finance Act, 2026, is crucial to ensure a cohesive and streamlined approach to import taxation. The DGFT has been proactive in amending the ITC (HS) Schedule to reflect the changes introduced by the Finance Act, 2026. This synchronization is essential to avoid conflicts and ambiguities in the application of import taxation provisions. As per Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the Central Government has the power to notify rules and regulations for the implementation of the Act, which includes the ITC (HS) Schedule.

The Supreme Court, in the case of Union of India vs. Delhi High Court Bar Association, has emphasized the importance of a harmonious and consistent approach to taxation. The Court held that the legislative intent behind the taxation laws should be given effect to, and any ambiguity or conflict should be resolved in a manner that promotes the overall objective of the legislation. In the context of the ITC (HS) import policy, this means that the DGFT's amendments to the Schedule should be aligned with the provisions of the Finance Act, 2026, to ensure a uniform and predictable taxation regime.
